questions.question: Explain the basic function of a 2-to-1 multiplexer. Describe its inputs, outputs, and the role of the select line.
keyPoints: A 2-to-1 MUX selects one of two input lines (I0 or I1) to be routed to the single output line (Y).
The selection is controlled by a single select line (S). The output Y = I0 when S=0, and Y = I1 when S=1.
It is the fundamental building block for larger multiplexers and is a core component in data selection and routing applications.

questions.question: Describe how you would construct a 4-to-1 multiplexer using only 2-to-1 multiplexers. Include a brief explanation of how the final circuit operates.
keyPoints: A 4-to-1 MUX requires two select lines. It can be built using three 2-to-1 MUXes in a hierarchical structure.
The first stage uses two 2-to-1 MUXes, each controlled by the least significant select bit (S0). They select between two of the four inputs (e.g., I0/I1 and I2/I3).

The second stage uses a third 2-to-1 MUX, controlled by the most significant select bit (S1). It selects the output from the first-stage MUX that corresponds to the desired input group, producing the final output.

questions.question: Multiplexers can be used to implement any Boolean function. Using a specific example of a 3-variable function (e.g., F(A,B,C) = Σ(1,3,5,6)), explain the process of using an 8-to-1 MUX as a look-up table for its implementation.
keyPoints: The three input variables (A, B, C) are connected to the three select lines of the 8-to-1 MUX. Each select line combination corresponds to one minterm of the truth table.

The data inputs (I0 to I7) of the MUX are set to the value of the function F for the corresponding minterm. For the example F = Σ(1,3,5,6), inputs I1, I3, I5, I6 are set to 1, and all others (I0, I2, I4, I7) are set to 0.
The output of the MUX (Y) is then the value of the function F for the current input combination on the select lines, effectively implementing the function by acting as a programmable truth table.
